name: Brussels Beer Project
vibe: Startup energy meets craft beer — the creative district's living room
why: Where the Dansaert crowd drinks something they can't get anywhere else. Their own brews, constantly rotating.

name: Moeder Lambic
vibe: The institution. 400+ beers, staff who actually know them all.
why: Brussels' original craft beer bar (2006). Pilgrimage spot for anyone serious about Belgian beer.

name: Fin de Siècle
vibe: Communal tables, chalkboard menu, zero pretension. Cash only.
why: No menu printed, no reservations taken, always packed. The most authentic Belgian dinner in the city.

name: Rebel
vibe: Low-intervention wines, rotating small plates, open late. Ixelles nightlife anchor.
why: By the chef behind Ötap — serious wine credentials with a bar atmosphere. Fills up late.

name: Fermento
vibe: Italian warmth, Belgian wine obsession. Wine is the only drink.
why: Near Flagey, reliably excellent wine list, regulars who come back every week. One of the best in the city.

name: Titulus
vibe: Elevated terrace, floor-to-ceiling bottles, sake section. Shop and bar in one.
why: The most curated natural wine selection in Brussels. Independent producers, ethical sourcing.

name: Scopa
vibe: Fresh-baked focaccia, counter dining, Mediterranean-forward. Lunch hotspot.
why: Opened and immediately became a neighbourhood staple. Fresh, fast, not a tourist in sight.

name: Drug Opera
vibe: Grand belle-époque room, classic Belgian cooking elevated. Feels special without being stuffy.
why: The rare Grand Place-area restaurant that isn't a tourist trap. Excellent waterzooi and Belgian classics.

name: Friture René
vibe: Old-school friture — frites, mussels, stoemp. The real thing since 1932.
why: Critics and chefs from across Europe make the trip. Fried Brussels on a plate.

name: Brasserie Verschueren
vibe: Art Nouveau interior, cheap beer, good frites. Unchanged for decades.
why: Step inside and it's 1952. Original fixtures, locals propping up the bar. A Brussels treasure.

name: Café De Markten
vibe: All-day café that turns into a bar. Design crowd, cultural events, good food.
why: Daytime remote work, evening drinks — one of the best all-rounders in the city.
address: 5 Rue du Vieux Marché aux Grains

name: JAT Café
vibe: Calm, plugs at every table, specialty coffee that doesn't disappoint.
why: The remote worker's secret weapon. Not on every list, consistently good coffee, reliable wifi.

name: Arion Café
vibe: Warm, neighbourhood, proper regional Belgian cooking without the tourist markup.
why: Waterzooi, vol-au-vent, seasonal game — executed properly. The Belgian cooking class you didn't know you needed.

name: Café Capitale
vibe: Clean, fast, excellent espresso. The EU quarter done properly.
why: Black Potion Coffee runs this. For when you need caffeine before a morning meeting.

name: Barracuda
vibe: Loud, beautiful, Italian group dining. Big Mamma's Brussels outpost.
why: You're going to wait. It's worth it. Fresh pasta, the anti-tourist Italian chain that somehow isn't a chain.

name: Le 203
vibe: Candlelit stone and brick, open-kitchen counter you can sit at, weekly-changing menu, Pacific-French-Asian in one room.
why: South African chef cooking in front of you, French front of house, no fixed menu. Get a counter seat and watch the kitchen work — one of the most interesting rooms in the city right now.

name: Moutarde
vibe: Midnight-blue velvet, dark wood, américain frites and Comté gougères until 1am.
why: Victor Roisin (of Gratin) does the classic brasserie right — zero-waste kitchen, real ingredients, open late.

name: Malō Coffee
vibe: Blue-green interiors near Tenbosch Park, NY-style bagels, precise espresso.
why: The specialty coffee shop that doesn't feel like it's trying. Artisan roasters, seasonal bagels, proper baristas.

name: De Hoef 1627
vibe: Old tiles, exposed beams, wood fire, Burgundy snails. Four generations of the same family.
why: Established as a restaurant in the 1950s in a 17th-century coaching inn. Grilled meats over fire, courtyard terrace, the real Belgian deal.

name: Koku Ramen
vibe: Counter seating, no reservations, queues out the door. Worth every minute.
why: Homemade tonkotsu broth, chicken oil, the real thing. Opens twice daily for short windows — if you can get a seat, you'll be back.
openingHours: Dinner from 19:00 (limited service windows, call ahead)

name: Café Belga
vibe: Enormous terrace on Flagey square, coffee at noon, gin at midnight. Brussels' most-loved institution.
why: The beating heart of Ixelles. On a sunny day, the square belongs to Café Belga. Every neighbourhood needs one — Brussels only has one.

name: Nuetnigenough
vibe: Timber and brass, 2,000+ Belgian beers on the menu. The nerdiest beer bar in the country.
why: Open since 1978. The beer list is a book. Ask the staff — they've read it.

name: Brasserie de la Senne
vibe: Industrial taproom at the source. Taras Boulba on draft, the way it was meant to be drunk.
why: Brussels' best-loved craft brewery in its natural habitat. Zinnebir, Stouterik, and rotating seasonals direct from the tank.

name: MOK
vibe: Brussels' favourite independent roaster. Single-origins, filter coffee, no fuss.
why: MOK supplies half the specialty cafés in Brussels — coming here is the source. Rotating pour-overs, excellent espresso, bread.

name: Belga Queen
vibe: Soaring glass vaults, 400-cover brasserie in a former clearing house. Grand is an understatement.
why: Antoine Pinto's masterpiece. The room alone is worth the visit — steak tartare and Belgian classics inside a cathedral of a building.

name: La Buvette
vibe: Terracotta walls, low light, natural wine and charcuterie until midnight. The Saint-Gilles soul.
why: A neighbourhood wine bar that became a Brussels institution. No pretension — just very good wine, very good plates.

name: Viva M'Boma
vibe: Former horse butcher. Ox cheek, tripe, sweet and sour rabbit. Nose-to-tail Belgian in a 19th-century setting.
why: Offal and tradition done right — the menu hasn't changed in 30 years because it doesn't need to.

name: Bar du Matin
vibe: All-day café that turns into music, DJs, and live performance at night. Start chill, maybe stay late.
why: Coffee at noon, terrace beer after work, DJ at midnight — the rare venue that gracefully moves through every register the same night.

name: Chez Biquet
vibe: Proper Brussels friterie. Queue outside, eat on the steps of the flea market.
why: Cash only, open since the 70s, frites fried twice in beef fat. The Marolles institution that hasn't changed and shouldn't.

name: Brasserie Ploegmans
vibe: Unchanged since 1930. Gueuze on tap, regulars who've been coming for decades.
why: The most Brussels bar in Brussels. Overlooking Place du Jeu de Balle — go Saturday morning after the flea market with a gueuze and you'll understand the city.

name: Fuse
vibe: Belgium's most famous techno club since 1994. Three floors, sound system, Saturday-night-into-Sunday-afternoon.
why: Where Brussels' electronic music scene has its capital. Sven Väth slot, international bookings, the city's nightlife institution.

name: À la Mort Subite
vibe: Tiled walls, marble tables, mirrors. Unchanged since 1928. Lambic, gueuze, kriek poured straight from the cask.
why: The most famous traditional café in Brussels. Tourist-busy but the staff are unbothered and the gueuze is exactly what you came for.

name: Le Cirio
vibe: Belle-époque glass canopy, polished brass, half-and-half (champagne + white wine) at midnight.
why: Open since 1886, somehow uncolonised by tourism. The room is the point — sit at the bar, order the half-and-half, look up.

name: Hortense
vibe: Low light, no menu, tell the bartender what you like. One of the world's 50 best.
why: Carlos Madriz built Brussels' most awarded cocktail bar in a tiny Châtelain room. Reservations essential — but you'll remember it.

name: L'Archiduc
vibe: Art Deco split-level bar with a grand piano. Live jazz on Saturdays. Ring the bell to get in.
why: Since 1937 and least-changed bar in Brussels. The brass, velvet, and Charlie Parker on Saturday make this one of the most beautiful rooms in Europe.

name: Peck 47
vibe: Eggs benedict, pancakes the size of a plate, queues every weekend.
why: Brussels' brunch destination since the early 2010s. Australian-style café food done well — the kind of breakfast that fixes a Sunday.

name: Goupil le Fol
vibe: Three floors of bric-à-brac, Edith Piaf on loop, fruit wine in tea cups. Like drinking inside someone's memory.
why: There's nothing else like it in Brussels — or anywhere. Climb the staircase, find a sofa, order a strawberry wine. People remember the night they went, not what they drank.

name: Estaminet du Kelderke
vibe: 17th-century brick cellar under the Grand Place. Carbonade flamande, stoemp, mussels-and-frites.
why: Yes, it's on the Grand Place. No, it's not a tourist trap — Brussels families and locals still eat here. The cellar setting is irreplaceable.

name: Batch
vibe: Nordic minimalism in Brussels. Open-faced sandwiches at lunch, small plates and natural wine at dinner, cocktails late.
why: Brunch crowd by day, dinner crowd by night, the same crew either way. Scandi-leaning food in a city full of brasseries — a different register that lands.

name: Le Coq
vibe: Wooden bar, tiled floor, regulars, cash only. Belgian beers the way they were drunk in 1960.
why: Cash only and proud of it. The list is Belgian classics — Westmalle, Orval, Rochefort, lambics — pulled by people who've poured them for decades. A real Brussels café.

name: Delirium Café
vibe: Guinness Book of Records for largest beer selection — 2,000+ beers across multiple floors. Tourist central. You have been warned.
why: Yes, it's busy. Yes, every guidebook lists it. It's also genuinely the only place on earth with 2,000 beers in stock. If a friend wants the show, bring them here — then leave for a real bar afterwards.

name: Old Boy
vibe: Gourmet Chinese, sharp and modern. The opposite of the city's old-school Chinese spots.
why: A precise modern Chinese kitchen — refined dishes, careful sourcing, the kind of meal where every plate is the point. Reservations essential.

name: Da Long Yi HotPot
vibe: Bubbling pots around the table, sweat-inducing Sichuan broths, the social meal Brussels was missing.
why: Proper hotpot — multiple broths, raw ingredients, everyone cooks at their own pace. Goes for hours, perfect when there are six of you and the weather is cold.

name: Bozar Restaurant
vibe: Two stars inside Victor Horta's Palais des Beaux-Arts. The room is the show — Art Deco curves, cathedral ceilings.
why: When the dinner has to mean something. Karen Torosyan's kitchen is at the top of the city; the setting is the closest you'll get to eating inside a piece of Brussels architectural history.

name: La Paix
vibe: Two stars across from the abattoirs. Cinematic neighbourhood, exacting kitchen.
why: Anderlecht institution the Brussels food world treats as serious business. Less obvious than the city-centre fancy rooms — the better reason to make the trip.

name: Le Chalet de la Forêt
vibe: Two stars at the edge of the Sonian Forest. Elegant without being stuffy — wood, light, and trees outside.
why: Pascal Devalkeneer's outpost on the green border of Brussels. The setting alone is the date — quiet, green, slow.

name: Comme chez Soi
vibe: Brussels' grand fine-dining institution. Art Nouveau room, generations of polish, classic French-Belgian cooking.
why: If you only do one classic Brussels fine-dining dinner in your life, this is the one. Open since 1926 and still serious about it.

name: La Villa in the Sky
vibe: One star, glass-cube dining room on top of the IT Tower. 120 metres up, 360° over Brussels.
why: The view is the date — the city laid out beneath you while a one-star kitchen works through a tasting menu. Showier than the more rooted fancy spots; that's the point.

name: C12
vibe: Below-ground concrete, immersive sound, audiovisual programming. Brussels' answer to Berghain-energy without trying to be it.
why: The artsier end of the city's electronic scene. Bookings lean serious — international DJs, longer nights, a crowd that actually came for the music.

name: UMI
vibe: Curated programming, art-music crossover, smaller crowd than the bigger clubs.
why: For the night where you care what's on the line-up rather than just being out. Different week to week — check the schedule before committing.

name: Mirano Continental
vibe: Former cinema turned massive nightclub. House and techno Fridays, hip-hop and dancehall Saturdays.
why: When the brief is 'proper club' — big room, packed dancefloor, mainstream edge. Less for the music nerds, more for the night out.

name: Bloody Louis
vibe: Avenue Louise nightclub energy. Loud, dressed-up, mainstream bookings, queue at the door.
why: Not the cool kids' choice but the room delivers what it promises: big tunes, big crowd, proper night out. Commercial in the best and worst senses.

name: The Dominican Grand Lounge
vibe: Hotel-bar polish, velvet seating, plush dim corners, open fire at the entrance. Drinks feel like an occasion.
why: When you want a cocktail somewhere that takes itself slightly seriously — and a fireplace that earns its corner. The hotel-bar register Brussels mostly lacks.

name: Poechenellekelder
vibe: Marionettes hanging from every beam, hundreds of Belgian beers, peanut shells on the floor. Across from Manneken-Pis but not a tourist trap.
why: Improbably the real deal — Belgians drink here, the beer list is encyclopedic, the puppets stare back. Only-in-Brussels energy.

name: Le Petit Canon
vibe: Tiny Flagey-adjacent wine bar. A handful of tables, a careful list, low light — the kind of place where you talk and don't shout.
why: When La Buvette or Rebel are full. Smaller, more intimate, staff who actually want to talk to you about what's in the glass.

name: Chalet Robinson
vibe: On an island in the Bois de la Cambre lake. A little electric boat takes you across.
why: Sunday lunch territory, winter walks + hot chocolate, summer terrace. The boat-crossing is the point — turns a meal into a small adventure.
address: Sentier de l'Embarcadère 1, Bois de la Cambre

name: Supra Bailly
vibe: Big terrace, democratic prices, long beer list. Rue du Bailli's open-house café.
why: Easy group venue — no one feels left out, no one's overpaying, the terrace fits a crew. The 'just meet at Supra' default.

name: La Famille
vibe: Bobo-leaning Rue du Bailli café-bar. The kind of place a quick drink becomes three.
why: Trendy without trying, casual without being scruffy. Good for unplanned weeknight social with the same crew that always shows up.

name: Dinghi
vibe: Cocktails, bitters, small plates, Place du Châtelain terrace. Aperitivo done right.
why: The most polished Châtelain pre-dinner spot — proper drinks, Italian-leaning bites, a little more thought than a regular bar.

name: Tarzan
vibe: Natural wine, small plates, Châtelain-cool without the try-hard. Understated and very good.
why: When you want a wine night that doesn't feel like an event. The list rotates, the food is sharp, the room is small enough to feel found.

name: OenoTK
vibe: Small Châtelain wine bar with character. Bottle-selection-first, drinking-second.
why: For when you actually want to taste something, not just have a glass. Knowledgeable list, low-key room, you'll walk out having learned a producer.

name: Chez Franz
vibe: Sunny terrace, brunch into drinks, the safe-pick Ixelles café for any time of day.
why: Doesn't try too hard. The 'meet me there in an hour' default — brunch, coffee, beer-after-work, dinner-with-a-friend. Everything works.

name: The Black Sheep
vibe: Modern Irish pub at Flagey. Guinness, sports on the screens, weekend brunch, big-table energy.
why: Where the expat Flagey crowd ends up for the football, the Guinness, and the bigger casual group. Reliable, friendly, no pretensions.

name: L'Amère à Boire
vibe: Flagey craft beer bar with a serious list. The Belga alternative when you actually care what you're drinking.
why: Real range — Belgian rarities, international craft, sour beers. Less crowd, more conversation about what's on tap.

name: MAGUIE
vibe: Saint-Gilles wine cellar with fresh seasonal food. Young, classy, small.
why: A wine bar where the menu rotates as fast as the bottles. Worth a small-group dinner — focused list, careful kitchen.

name: Moeder Lambic Fontainas
vibe: The central branch — same encyclopedic Belgian beer list, easier to find, terrace on Place Fontainas in summer.
why: Quality beer in the city centre without the tourist gloss of Delirium. When you're heading somewhere after dinner and need a proper pre-drink.

name: Life Is Beautiful
vibe: Dansaert cocktail bar that takes the room as seriously as the drinks. Tasteful, social, never loud for the sake of it.
why: The cocktail-bar alternative to Hortense — slightly easier to walk into, equally considered. Date or a group of four both work.

name: The Eight Rooftop
vibe: Eighth-floor rooftop with 180° views over Brussels. Cocktails, sunset crowd, a proper occasion drink.
why: When you want the view to be the night. Pre-dinner showpiece for friends visiting; works as a fancy first date too.
